content: Obesity is a complex chronic disease characterized by abnormal or excessive fat accumulation that presents risk to health. WHO defines obesity as BMI ≥30 kg/m², with class I (30-34.9), class II (35-39.9), and class III (≥40, formerly 'morbid obesity'). However, BMI has limitations—it doesn't distinguish fat from muscle mass, varies by ethnicity (lower cutoffs for Asian populations), and doesn't capture fat distribution (visceral adiposity more metabolically harmful). Global obesity has nearly tripled since 1975—13% of adults obese, 39% overweight in 2016. Obesity causes include: genetic factors (heritability 40-70%), epigenetic changes, obesogenic environment (calorie-dense food availability, sedentary lifestyles), socioeconomic factors, medications, sleep disruption, and endocrine disruptors. Health consequences include type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, multiple cancers, osteoarthritis, and mental health conditions.
